The morning light came in through the high narrow window in long pale bars, and for a moment the room felt almost peaceful. The traveller sat at the table with his hands folded and his eyes closed, and he listened to the small sounds of the house waking around him. A door opened and closed somewhere on the floor below. A kettle began to whistle. Outside, on the long gravel drive, a single bird repeated the same three notes over and over, as though it were trying to remember the rest of the song and could not. He did not move. He had learned, in the years since he came back, that the first hour of the day was the most fragile, and that any sudden motion could break the careful balance he had built for himself in the night.

When he finally opened his eyes, he opened them slowly, as if afraid of what he might see. The room was the same as it had been yesterday and the day before. The faded green wallpaper, the brass lamp with the cracked shade, the framed map of a country that no longer existed. On the table in front of him there was a small leather notebook, a fountain pen with the cap chewed at one end, and a cup of coffee that had gone cold while he was thinking. He picked up the pen first. He always picked up the pen first. It was a kind of promise to himself, a daily ritual: before anything else, write one sentence. It did not have to be a good sentence. It did not have to be true. It only had to be his.

Today, however, the sentence would not come. He sat with the pen poised above the blank page for what felt like a long time, and the bird outside finished its three notes again and again, and the kettle in the kitchen reached its full pitch and was silenced by someone he could not see. He thought about the letter that had arrived the previous evening, and about the name written on it in a hand he had not seen for nineteen years. He thought about what it meant that the letter had found him here, in a house he had taken under another name, in a town that did not appear on most maps. He thought about the woman who must have written it, and about the small clear handwriting that had not changed at all, and about the way she had always pressed too hard with her pen so that the back of every page was a kind of negative of the front.

He set the pen down without writing anything, and he picked up the cup of coffee, and he drank what was left of it in two swallows, even though it was cold and bitter. He had decided, sometime in the night without quite admitting it to himself, that he was going to go. He did not know yet how he was going to go, or what he would say when he arrived, or whether he would be welcome. He only knew that he could not stay in this room any longer, with the letter on the table and his name on the envelope, pretending he had not read it. He stood up, and the chair scraped against the wooden floor with a small unhappy sound. He took the letter from his coat pocket and folded it again along the same creases and put it back. Then he went to the wardrobe and opened it and looked for a long moment at the few things he owned.

The journey took most of the day. He took a train from the small station at the bottom of the hill, and he watched the country roll past the window in a slow, even procession of fields and stone walls and grey villages. He had bought a newspaper at the station but he did not read it. He held it on his knee with both hands as though it were a kind of shield, and he looked out at the country, and he tried to remember exactly what the house had looked like the last time he had seen it. He could remember the gate, and the long avenue of beech trees, and the way the gravel made a particular sound under the wheels of a carriage. He could not remember the colour of the door, which surprised him and bothered him in a way he could not quite explain.

At the second station he changed trains, and at the third he hired a car. The driver was an older man with a thick scarf and a habit of clearing his throat before each sentence. He asked the traveller, in a polite, indirect way, what business he had at the house. The traveller said that he was an old friend of the family. The driver nodded once, as if this answer satisfied him completely, and did not ask anything more. They drove for half an hour along a narrow road between high hedges, and then the hedges fell away on the left, and the traveller saw the gate, and he asked the driver to stop a little before they reached it. He paid the man and got out of the car and stood for a long time on the verge of the road, with his small bag at his feet and his coat buttoned all the way to the throat.

The house had not changed. That was the first thing he noticed, and it surprised him almost more than anything else that day. The beech trees were taller, and the gravel of the avenue had been recently raked, and a new lamp had been fitted by the front door. But the shape of the house was exactly as he remembered it, and the windows on the upper floor were open the way they had always been open in the early evening, and there was a thin column of smoke rising from the chimney at the western end, which meant that someone was sitting in the small library and had lit the fire there for company. He thought, quite clearly and quite calmly, that he could still turn around. He thought that the driver was probably not yet out of sight. He thought of all the things he had practised saying on the train, and he understood, with a small private pang, that he was not going to say any of them.
